intro: Python focuses on explicit, unambiguous syntax with strong architectural tooling across diverse domain scopes, while Ruby prioritizes developer happiness and extreme runtime flexibility through highly expressive, block-centric object dynamics. Developers translate scripts to Ruby to fit within mature web ecosystems like Ruby on Rails or to exploit advanced runtime metaprogramming patterns. This porting workflow maps clean indentation structures into flexible, block-driven code statements where everything is explicitly an object.

diffs.from: Strongly, dynamically typed. Enforces explicit runtime constraints, rejecting operations across conflicting variables.
diffs.to: Strongly, dynamically typed. Uses pure duck typing where an object's capabilities are determined by its responding methods at runtime.

diffs.from: Whitespace indentation rules determine code execution blocks, avoiding block-terminating keyword flags.
diffs.to: Keyword-enclosed statement blocks (def...end) that ignore whitespace indentation layout, supporting optional method parentheses.

diffs.from: Managed via reference counting parameters operating in tandem with an internal cyclical reclamation engine.
diffs.to: Automated object tracking utilizing a global mark-and-sweep garbage collection framework tuned for high object creation rates.

diffs.from: Cooperative concurrency architecture driven by native async/await keywords and an integrated asyncio system manager.
diffs.to: Orchestrated via Fiber objects, Ractor isolation models, or traditional thread instances, avoiding strict single-loop configurations.

diffs.from: Broad system toolkit containing extensive packages for network engineering, math tracking, and automation script structures.
diffs.to: Rich object-oriented toolkit specializing in elegant string manipulation, collection filtering, and meta-object creation utilities.

examples.title: Iterating collections with block closures
examples.description: Compares an explicit Python loop construct against Ruby's idiomatic block iterator method.
examples.toCode:
items = [1, 2, 3]
items.each do |x|
  puts x * 2
end

examples.title: Dynamic method intercept processing
examples.description: Shows the difference between overriding attribute hooks versus capturing undefined method execution triggers dynamically.
examples.toCode:
class Proxy
  def method_missing(name, *args)
    "Missing: #{name}"
  end
end

tips.title: Omit Parentheses on Idiomatic Calls
tips.body: Ruby permits dropping parentheses around method arguments when the call is clear; practice this construct to align with idiomatic styling.

tips.title: Rely on Implicit Returns
tips.body: Ruby methods automatically return the evaluation results of their final statement block line; omit explicit return keywords.

tips.title: Leverage Blocks and Procs
tips.body: Replace explicit lambda iterations or functional helper maps with Ruby's block structures (do...end) passed natively into target method routines.

tips.title: Understand True Object Foundations
tips.body: In Ruby, every value—including basic primitives like numbers and booleans—is an instantiation of a class and responds to method calls.

faqs.question: What is the Ruby equivalent to Python's dictionary structure?
faqs.answer: Ruby uses the `Hash` class, which frequently utilizes symbols (`key: value` or `:key => value`) as lightweight, highly optimized index keys.

faqs.question: How do Python decorators map to Ruby structures?
faqs.answer: Ruby achieves decoration using metaprogramming patterns, such as class macro helper methods or hooking into lifecycle hooks like `method_added`.

faqs.question: Are Ruby strings mutable like Python strings?
faqs.answer: No, Ruby strings are mutable by default. You can modify them inline unless you explicitly freeze them or declare `# frozen_string_literal: true`.
